response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
Alright, so I've been thinking a lot about how to handle conversations around COVID vaccines lately. As someone who's not exactly an expert in the medical field but has done my fair share of reading up on it because we're living through this pandemic thing that just keeps going on and on, I gotta say managing these talks can be tricky.

When people start bringing up healthcare and vaccines at the dinner table or online groups where I hang out daily scrolling down from hours ago now feels like navigating a minefield sometimes. My dad only went to primary school so health topics used to make my head spin more than usual, but with my mom having that vocational diploma she might offer some practical insights.

Because of my depression diagnosis and being unemployed right now in New York City during this time where everyone is talking anyway, these discussions hit especially hard for me. I've got moderate agreeableness scores meaning I don't want to rock the boat too much but sometimes getting stuck on misinformation feels inevitable when you're tired all the time like I am.

My low neuroticism helps keep my cool during debates though because otherwise social media could drive me crazy with everyone having strong opinions. But what works for me might not work if someone else is dealing with higher stress levels here.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I approached this by considering how personal traits like depression and being unemployed impact perspective on these discussions, while also thinking about how low neuroticism helps manage emotional responses. My education level (upper secondary) means I rely more on accessible sources rather than deep medical expertise.

I imagined the context of daily social media scrolling in New York where debates are frequent and public health matters affect everyone directly. The vocational diploma from my mom adds another layer to consider how different educational backgrounds shape viewpoints.
